title: Light the Candle When You Walk In
subtitle: A one-minute evening ritual for marking the end of the work day at home
excerpt: A one-minute evening ritual for marking the transition from the working day to the home. The ritual is a single physical act: lighting a candle as you walk in. The act creates a threshold.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Uses the threshold ritual principle from somatic practice.

practice.whenToUse: Use at the end of the work day as a physical signal that the work day is over. Good when working from home makes the boundary between work and rest unclear.

content.text: A one-minute ritual for the end of the work day. The transition from working to home-mode is easier when there is a physical act marking it. A candle is specific enough to be a signal, simple enough to do every day.
New to rituals as a practice? Read
As you come in from the working day, or at the point when you are done working if you work from home, go to wherever you keep a candle. A fixed spot is better than a different place each time.
Light it. Do not do anything else at the same time.
Watch the flame for ten seconds before you move on.

The act does not need any additional steps or words. The consistency is the practice: doing it every evening makes the nervous system start to register the moment of lighting as the point where work is done.
Original to homemade.education. It draws on body-based practice and the idea of threshold rituals.
